Ministry of Education

TEACHING MODULE 
FORM 1-TERM THREE 
 
 
 
 
Published by Curriculum Development Centre 
Lusaka 
2025

2 
 
 
Republic of Zambia 
 
Ministry of Education

The Curriculum Development Centre 
 
 
TEACHING MODULE 
FORM 1 
TERM THREE

3 
 
@ Curriculum Development Centre 2025  
All rights reserved. No parts of this publication may be reproduced, stored in a retrieval system 
or transmitted by electronic, mechanical, photocopying, recording or other means, without prior 
permission of the Curriculum Development Centre

4 
 
 
Authors  
Stephen Moyo:      Curriculum Development Specialist - English Language, CDC, Lusaka.  
Nawa Mushiba:     Curriculum Development Specialist - English Language, CDC, Lusaka.  
Anderson Lwimbo: Head Teacher, Shimukuni Secondary School, Chibombo. 
Charles Kabungo:  Education Standards Officer, Kalulushi.  
John Moono:        Teacher, Mbala Boarding Secondary School, Mbala. 
Mathew Kang’ombe: Lecturer, Chalimbana University, Chongwe. 
Monde Sinyangwe:  Education Standards Officer, Luanshya. 
Monica Kamwengo-Imasiku:  Teacher, Roma Girls Secondary School, Lusaka. 
Mushaukwa Mubuka-Matale:  Deputy Head Teacher, Kaunda Square Secondary School, Lusaka. 
Namushi Situtu:     Senior College Lecturer, Chipata College of Education, Chipata.

5 
 
PREFACE  
The advent of the Competence-Based Curriculum (CBC) heralds a new era of dynamic and 
practical learning experiences designed to equip learners with the 21st century skills. It marks a 
trans-formative step in improving education quality and relevance.  
The Literature in English Teaching Module for Ordinary level has been designed in line with the 
Competence Based Education principles which seek to link education to real life experiences. 
The General Competences provided in the 2023 Zambia Curriculum Framework are linked to the 
content of this module.  
The module aims to support teachers of Literature in English in the implementation of the CBC 
in the absence of approved textbooks. It therefore reflects the Ministry’s unwavering 
commitment to making this journey as smooth as possible for teachers. However, teachers are 
encouraged to supplement this module with their own research and innovations in order to 
address any gaps that may arise during implementation. 
I hope teachers will make good use of this resource.  
 
Kelvin Mambwe (PhD.) 
Permanent Secretary- Educational Services 
MINISTRY OF EDUCATION

6 
 
Acknowledgement 
The Literature in English Teaching Module for Ordinary Level has been developed through a 
consultative and participatory process. Many individuals, institutions and organizations were 
consulted and accorded an opportunity to make proposals about the content of this teaching 
module. I would, therefore, like to express my profound gratitude to individuals, institutions and 
organizations that provided the financial and technical input to the successful development of 
this teaching module. These include; Teachers, Lecturers from Colleges of Education and 
Universities, Subject Associations, Civil Society Organizations, and Cooperating Partners. 
Finally, I appreciate the commitment and hard work of the staff at the Directorate of Curriculum 
Development in ensuring the successful completion of the Teaching Module. 
 
Charles Ndakala, (Dr.) 
Director - Curriculum Development 
MINISTRY OF EDUCATION

9 
 
INTRODUCTION  
The Literature in English Teaching Module for Ordinary level has been developed to support 
teachers in the effective delivery of Literature in English lessons under the reformed curriculum. 
The module is developed based on the approved 2024 Competence-based Literature in English 
syllabus. It aims to bridge the gap created by the absence of approved textbooks and to equip 
educators with activities and assessments tailored to the learners’ level and needs. 
 
The Ministry understands the challenges that come with transitioning to a new curriculum that is 
Competence-Based. The choice of the Competence-Based Curriculum (CBC) marks a 
transformative step in improving education quality and relevance. This module reflects the 
Ministry’s unwavering commitment to making this journey as smooth as possible for teachers, 
by providing structured guidance, suggested activities, and formative assessments that align with 
the CBC’s objectives. However, teachers are encouraged to supplement this module with their 
own research and innovations in order to address any gaps that may arise during implementation. 
The suggested activities and assessments provided in this module are designed to foster practical 
and critical thinking skills in learners while ensuring relevance and adaptability to different 
school contexts. Teachers are encouraged to tailor these pedagogical skills to their unique 
classroom dynamics and to explore alternative, acceptable approaches which are learner centred. 
This flexibility is key to ensuring that all learners, regardless of their environment, benefit from 
meaningful and impactful Curriculum. 
 
It is envisaged that this module will serve as a valuable resource for teachers as they navigate the 
new Competence-Based Curriculum, enhancing their ability to inspire and equip learners with 
essential literary knowledge, skills and positive attitudes desired to create a vibrant and 
responsive education system.

10 
 
 
HOW THE MODULE SHOULD BE USED 
This module contains the content scheduled for teaching in the third term of Form 1 Literature in 
English. It is divided into topics and sub-topics for different components. The content provided 
in this module are not scripted lessons but should be used to come up with lessons. When 
preparing lessons, you should draw content from different sources. As you prepare a lesson you 
must select appropriate teaching and learning activities as well as suggested teaching points. 
Furthermore, the activities provided for each competence are not meant to be exhausted in one 
lesson. These can be done in different lessons. Note however that, this module is meant to guide 
the teacher and not a resource for learners. 
 
SUGGESTED TEACHING METHODOLOGY 
Generally, lesson delivery should follow a variety of teaching methods and techniques such as 
class discussion, role play, simulation, dramatization, multi-media integration and project-based 
learning.

11 
 
TERM 3-LITERATURE IN ENGLISH- TEACHING AND LEARNING 
ACTIVITIES 
TOPIC: 1.2.2 WRITTEN LITERATURE 
 
INTRODUCTION 
Written Literature has a number of genres that appeal to the mind of the audience. One such 
genre is drama. Drama is a genre of literature and performance that involves a serious emotional, 
or thought-provoking narrative, presented in front of the audience. 
GENERAL COMPETENCES 
The learners will exhibit the following competences: 
 Analytical Thinking: grasp and breakdown information and effectively share with others. 
 Collaboration: Learners to participate fully in groups and play the assigned roles or 
tasks. 
 Communication: Effectively share one’s thoughts and emotions, and understand others. 
 Critical Thinking: Analyse texts, to improve the ability to understand.  
 
 Emotional Intelligence: communicate and relate effectively with others 
KEY TERMS / VOCABULARY 
Some key terms/vocabulary to be introduced in this sub-topic include: 
 Script- piece of writing meant to be performed.  
 Analyse – to examine something in detail. 
 Genre – type of artistic work that shares common characteristics. 
 Theme – a central idea in a literary work 
 Audience – a group of people before whom drama is performed. 
 Emotions – strong feelings evoked in people as they watch or listen to drama

12 
 
 
SUB TOPIC: 1.2.2.3. DRAMA  
 
Overview 
In this sub topic, the definition of drama and its key elements such as Plot, Character, 
Script/Play, Stage Directions and Audience will be discussed. Learners will be expected to 
perform plays.  
Specific Competence – Learners to: 
1.2.2.3.1. Demonstrate understanding of drama. 
Learning Activity 1a Discussing Drama and its elements (script meant to be 
performed on stage: plot, character (actor), dialogue, setting, conflict, staging, theme, 
symbolism, irony…) 
 Introduce drama and its elements to the learners and how drama can be performed as 
provided in the excerpt below.  
 
“The Price of a Shortcut” 
(Stage Director- provides stage directions in this play) 
Characters (actors) 
Mwamba – A Form 1 learner, smart but tempted by shortcuts. 
Chipego – Mwamba’s best friend, honest and hardworking. 
Teacher Lubasi – Their class teacher. 
Mr. Phiri – A corrupt council official. 
Mama Mwansa – Mwamba’s mother. 
Policeman  
 
Scene 1: At School – Classroom  
It is another day at Kalulu Basic School. Examinations are coming up and everyone is 
preparing—well, almost everyone. 
(Mwamba and Chipego are seated at their desks. Chipego is reading, Mwamba is playing 
with his phone.) 
Chipego: Mwamba, are you not going to revise? The exams are next week! 
Mwamba: Ah, Chipego, why stress? I heard my uncle can get me the examination paper 
in advance. Just a little something for the “officer.” 
Chipego: You mean… cheat? That is not right, Mwamba! 
Mwamba: Who cares? Even big people steal money and get away with it. What is the 
point of suffering? 
Exit 
But what Mwamba does not know is that shortcuts have consequences.

13 
 
 
Scene 2: Mwamba’s Home – Evening 
(Mwamba and Mama Mwansa are seated. He is excited.) 
Mwamba: Mama, I found a way to pass my exams easily! Uncle Phiri says he can get the 
paper for me—just K500. 
Mama Mwansa: Mwamba! That is corruption. That is like stealing. You must pass with 
your own effort. 
Mwamba: But everyone does it! 
Mama Mwansa: That does not make it right. Corruption is like a disease. It destroys our 
future, little by little. 
Exit 
 
Scene 3: Council Office – Daytime 
(Mr. Phiri sits behind a desk. Mwamba sneaks in.) 
Mr. Phiri: Ah, young Mwamba. Do you have the money? 
Mwamba: (nervously) Yes, Uncle… here is the K500. 
(As Mwamba hands over the money, a Policeman walks in.) 
Policeman: Caught you! We have been tracking your corruption. Both of you are under 
arrest. 
Mwamba: No! I just wanted to pass… 
Exit 
And just like that, Mwamba’s shortcut leads him into a bigger problem. But this is not the 
end. 
Scene 4: Classroom – After a Week 
(Teacher Lubasi addresses the class.) 
Teacher Lubasi: Class, I want you to remember: corruption is not just about stealing 
money. It’s also cheating, bribing, or doing wrong to get ahead. 
Chipego: So what happened to Mwamba? 
Teacher Lubasi: He is learning the hard way. But we hope he will change. The future of 
Zambia depends on young people like you doing the right thing. 
Cast: (Come forward and say together) “Say no to corruption!” 
(cast exits) 
End of Play 
 
Let us all say no to corruption—at school, at home, and everywhere. A better Zambia 
starts with you. 
 
 Lead a class discussion on drama and its elements.  
Consolidate the activity by highlighting the following:   
 Drama is a genre of literature that is intended to be performed by actors in front of an 
audience. It can also be experienced through reading scripts. 
 Here are key elements of drama:

14 
 
a. Plot- the sequence of events in a play.  
b. Characters (Actors)-performers of actions in a play.  
c. Dialogue-conversations between two characters.   
d. Conflict- the problem or struggle that the character encounters which drives the plot and 
creates tension in the play. 
e. Theme- the central or main idea in a play.  
f. Setting-The time, place and social context in which the drama takes place.  
g. Staging-how the play is presented on stage (set design). 
h. Stage Directions-Instructions given by the playwright for the performance. They 
describe how actors should move, speak or interact with props and other actors on stage. 
i. Symbolism-Objects, actions or characters that represent deeper meaning.  
j. Irony- statements are directly opposite of what is actually meant. 
 
Elements of Drama in the Script – The Price of a Shortcut 
Conflict 
The central conflict is between Mama Mwansa, who values integrity, and Mwamba, who 
embodies corruption. The clash between right and wrong drives the narrative. 
Characterization 
Mama Mwansa represents honesty, hard work, and moral strength. Mwamba represents greed, 
manipulation, and dishonesty. 
Chipego represents the innocent people who suffer because of corruption, while the Policeman 
shows that not everyone in the system is corrupt. 
Dialogue 
The dialogue reveals the characters’ beliefs, motivations, and personalities. Mwamba persuasive 
tactics contrast with Mama Mwansa firm resolve. 
Setting 
The dialogues take place in different place in different places like the Classroom, Mwamba’s 
Home and the Council Office.  
Resolution 
The resolution shows that standing up for what is right, even against powerful forces, can lead to 
positive change. Mama Mwansa victory is small but meaningful. 
Theme  
This script not only highlights the consequences of corruption but also shows how courage, 
integrity, and standing up for what is right can have a lasting impact. It also serves as a lesson for 
young people on the importance of moral values in society.

15 
 
Suggested Teaching and Learning Material: Flip Charts, Markers, Written Texts …   
Expected Standard: Understanding of Drama demonstrated adequately 
 
                
                        Assessment Activity 
Assign topics to learners on which they can write scripts to be acted  in the next lesson  
 
Summary  
Conclude the lesson by highlighting the following; 
 Drama is a genre of literature that is intended to be performed by actors in front of an 
audience. It can also be experienced through reading scripts. 
 Plot- the sequence of events in a play.  
 Characters (Actors)-performers of actions in a play.  
 Dialogue-conversations between two characters.   
 Conflict- the problem or struggle that the character encounters which drives the plot 
and creates tension in the play. 
 Theme- the central or main idea in a play.  
 Setting-The time, place and social context in which the drama takes place.  
 Staging- how the play is presented on stage (set design).  
 Stage Directions-Instructions given by the playwright for the performance. They 
describe how actors should move, speak or interact with props and other actors on 
stage. 
 Symbolism-Objects, actions or characters that represent deeper meaning.  
 Irony- statements are directly opposite of what is actually meant 
 
Specific Competence: Learners to 
 1.2.2.3.2. Perform Plays 
 
Learning activity 1a Performing Plays (on cross cutting themes such as gender, climate 
change, corruption, governance...). 
 Let the learners present the scripts that they wrote as assigned to them in the previous 
lesson.  
 Allow the groups to perform their plays. 
 Conduct a plenary session.

16 
 
Consolidate the activity by highlighting the following:  
 
 Learners shall learn and acquire the skill of performing plays. Guidelines shall be given 
to learners on how to act in a play such as: 
 Costume- clothing and accessories worn by actors to portray characters in a play. 
 Voice projection-clarity and audibility of the actor’s voice.  
 Body movement- deliberate use of an actor’s physical self to convey meaning, feelings 
and emotions. They include posture, gestures, facial expression and gait. 
 Facial expression- use of the face to show emotions, thoughts and reactions of a 
character. 
 Gestures- small movements used with any part of the body to communicate meaning, 
feelings or actions to an audience. Proper use of gestures can amplify a point, put 
emphasis and indicate the emotions of characters.  
 Properties (Props)- objects that actors handle or use on stage. 
 Staging- how the play is presented on stage (set design).  
 Stage Directions-Instructions given by the playwright for the performance. They describe 
how actors should move, speak or interact with props and other actors on stage. 
 Turn-taking-the way actors in a conversation take turns speaking, listening and 
responding.  
 
Suggested Teaching and Learning Materials: Recorded plays, costume, flip charts, markers… 
Expected Standard: Plays performed skillfully. 
 
  
Assessment Activity 
 Assign learners specific roles that they will act out in a play on any cross-cutting themes.  
 
Summary 
Conclude the lesson by highlighting the following: 
 Costume- clothing and accessories worn by actors to portray characters in a play. 
 Voice projection-clarity and audibility of the actor’s voice.  
 Body movement- deliberate use of an actor’s physical self to convey meaning, feelings 
and emotions. They include posture, gestures, facial expression and gait. 
 Facial expression- use of the face to show emotions, thoughts and reactions of a 
character. 
 Gestures- small movements used with any part of the body to communicate meaning, 
feelings or actions to an audience. Proper use of gestures can amplify a point, put 
emphasis and indicate the emotions of characters.  
 Properties (Props)- objects that actors handle or use on stage.  
 Staging- how the play is presented on stage (set design).

17 
 
 Stage Directions-Instructions given by the playwright for the performance. They describe 
how actors should move, speak or interact with props and other actors on stage. 
 Turn-taking-the way actors in a conversation take turns speaking, listening and 
responding.  
 
SUB-TOPIC- 1.2.2.4. POETRY 
 
Overview 
Poetry is a general term used for poems as a genre of literature. Poems are used to express ideas 
and emotions. This sub topic discusses poetry, its elements and how to perform and review 
poems. 
Specific Competence- Learners to: 
1.2.2.4.1. Demonstrate understanding of Poetry 
Learning Activity 1a Discussing poetry and basic elements of poetry (piece of    
writing that uses aesthetic language: line, verse, stanza…) 
 Introduce poetry and its elements to the learners and how poems can be performed and 
reviewed as provided in the excerpt below.  
 
Zambia is Getting Warmer 
The sun beats down with fiery light, 
Crops dry up, no rain in sight. 
Trees are cut, the forests thin, 
The heat grows strong; it burns the skin. 
The Zambezi’s flow is slow and low, 
Fields go bare where maize won’t grow. 
 
Birds still call from dying trees, 
While floods wash farms with rising streams. 
Clouds bring storms, then disappear, 
The weather shifts from year to year. 
We must act and make things clear— 
Protect our land or lose it, dear. 
 
 Ask learners to state emotions and ideas on which poems can be based. (Teacher to guide 
learners on thematic areas of poetry) 
 Lead a class discussion on what a poem is and its elements

18 
 
 
Consolidate the activity by highlighting the following: 
 Poetry- is a form of literature that uses rhythmic and figurative language to express 
emotions and ideas. 
 A poem is a literary work that expresses emotions and ideas.  
 Poems are usually Didactic or Aesthetic. Didactic is a literary work that intends to deliver 
a lesson and Aesthetic is an appreciation of beauty of literary works. 
Elements of poetry 
 These are fixed components of a poem. They are important parts of a piece of poetry that 
a poet cannot do without.  
Structural elements of a poem 
 Form- the structure of the poem.  
 Stanza: a division of a poem consisting of two or more lines arranged together as a unit. 
 Line: a unit of writing into which a poem or play is divided. 
 Verse: a collection of lines with a metrical rhythm in a poem.  
 Rhythm- the pattern of stressed and unstressed syllables in lines of poetry; it creates a 
musical quality. 
 Rhyme- the repetition of similar sounds in two or more words. 
 Plot, setting, characters and characterization: (Narrative Poetry)- See notes on short 
stories 
 
Poetic Devices  
 Mood: the disposition of mind and feelings. We can refer to the moods of anger, 
happiness, sadness that characterize a novel or part of it. 
 Imagery- descriptive language that appeals to the senses 
 Language- the poet’s word choice including figurative language 
 Tone- the poet’s attitude towards the subject or audience.  
 Alliteration- repetition of initial consonant sounds  
 Assonance- repetition of vowel sound  
 Consonance- repetition of consonant sounds within or at the end of words  
 Onomatopoeia- words that imitate sounds  
 
Suggested Teaching and Learning Material: Radio, recorded poems, flip charts, markers, 
whistles and poetry anthologies. 
Expected Standard: Understanding of poetry demonstrated adequately. 
Assessment Activities 
1. Provide a poem and ask learners to identify the elements of poetry in that poem. 
2. Homework: Assign poems to learners to be performed in the next lesson.

19 
 
Summary 
Conclude the lesson by highlighting the following: 
 Poetry- is a form of literature that uses rhythmic and figurative language to express 
emotions and ideas. 
 A poem is a literary work that expresses emotions and ideas.  
 Poems are usually Didactic or Aesthetic. Didactic is a literary work that intends to deliver 
a lesson and Aesthetic is an appreciation of beauty of literary works. 
 These are fixed components of a poem. They are important parts of a piece of poetry that 
a poet cannot do without.  
 
Structural elements of a poem 
 Form- the structure of a poem.  
 Stanza: a division of a poem consisting of two or more lines arranged together as a unit. 
 Line: a unit of writing into which a poem or play is divided. 
 Verse: a collection of lines with a metrical rhythm in a poem.  
 Rhythm- the pattern of stressed and unstressed syllables in lines of poetry; it creates a 
musical quality. 
 Rhyme- the repetition of similar sounds in two or more words at the end of a line. 
 Plot, setting, characters and characterization (Narrative Poetry)- See notes on short 
stories. 
 
Poetic Devices  
 Mood: the disposition of mind and feelings. We can refer to the moods of anger, 
happiness, sadness that characterize a poem or part of it. 
 Imagery- descriptive language that appeals to the senses 
 Language- the poet’s word choice including figurative language 
 Tone- the poet’s attitude towards the subject or audience.  
 Alliteration- repetition of initial consonant sounds  
 Assonance- repetition of vowel sound  
 Consonance- repetition of consonant sounds within or at the end of words  
 Onomatopoeia- words that imitate sounds

20 
 
Specific Competence: Learners to  
  1.2.2.4.2. Perform Poems 
Activity 1 d- Performing Poems (poems…on cross-cutting issues; drug abuse, 
climate change, corruption, human rights, teenage pregnancies…) 
 Play a poem for the learners to listen to.  
 Lead a discussion on how to perform poems on different cross-cutting issues. (drug abuse, 
climate change, corruption, human rights, teenage pregnancies…) 
 Put learners in groups, ask them to perform poems on different cross-cutting issues given 
in the previous activity. (drug abuse, climate change, corruption, human rights, teenage 
pregnancies…) 
 Lead a discussion on reviewing performed poems (Content and meaning, poetic devices 
and techniques, structure and organisation…) 
  
Consolidate the activity by highlighting key aspects of how poems are performed and 
reviewed: 
Performing poetry involves bringing a poem to life through vocal expression, movement, and 
engagement with an audience. 
Performing poems focuses on content, meaning, poetic devices and structural organization. 
 Content and Meaning-The content of a performed poem often reflects personal 
experiences, cultural identity, historical themes, or contemporary social issues. 
 Poetic Devices: Effective poetry performances emphasize literary devices such as:   
Imagery-Vivid descriptions that engage the audience’s senses.  
Repetition-Reinforces key messages and creates rhythm. 
Alliteration and Assonance-Enhance the musicality of a poem.  
Figurative Language-Add depth to meaning.  
 
 Performing poems or performance poetry involves delivering poetry aloud with emphasis 
on:  
vocal delivery, gestures and body language to create a dynamic and engaging experience 
for the audience.  
It is a form of artistic expression that combines poetry, storytelling and performance. 
  
Suggested Teaching and Learning Material:  Radio, Computer, Projector, Flipcharts, 
Markers… 
 
Expected Standard: Poems performed skillfully.

21 
 
Assessment Activity 
1. In groups, assign learners to perform at least one poem.  
Summary  
 Conclude the lesson by highlighting the following: 
 Content and Meaning-The content of a performed poem often reflects personal 
experiences, cultural identity, historical themes, or contemporary social issues. 
 Poetic Devices: Effective poetry performances emphasize literary devices such as 
 Imagery-Vivid descriptions that engage the audience’s senses.  
 Repetition-Reinforces key messages and creates rhythm. 
 Alliteration and Assonance-Enhance the musicality of a poem.  
 Figurative Language-Add depth to meaning.  
 
 Performing poems or performance poetry involves delivering poetry aloud with emphasis 
on:  
 Vocal delivery, gestures and body language to create a dynamic and engaging 
experience for the audience.  
 It is a form of artistic expression that combines poetry, storytelling and performance.

22

23 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
SAMPLE LESSON PLAN 
 
NAME OF TEACHER:  ………………………..             DATE: 13/ 09/ 2025

24 
 
CLASS: Form 1  
 
 
 
 
         TIME: 07:00-08:20                                                       
SUBJECT: Literature in English  
 
         DURATION: 80 minutes 
TOPIC: 1.2.2 Written Literature                                     TOTAL NO. OF LEARNERS: 60 
SUB-TOPIC: 1.2.2.4 Poetry  
 
 
 
 
GENERAL COMPETENCES:  
 Collaboration: Learners to participate fully in groups and play the assigned roles or tasks. 
 Communication: Effectively share one’s thoughts and emotions, and understand others. 
 
SPECIFIC COMPETENCES: 1.2.2.4.1. Demonstrate understanding of Poetry 
RATIONALE: This lesson is on poetry. This is particularly on form, line and stanza. 
Discussion, collaboration, question and answer will be used in order to develop learners’ 
understanding of poetry. This is the first lesson in a series of three in a week. 
PRIOR KNOWLEDGE: Learners have general understanding of poetry. 
REFERENCES:  
 2024 Literature in English Syllabus Form 1 pages 8 – 9. 
 2025 Literature in English Teaching Module Form 1 Term 3 page…  
  
LEARNING ENVIRONMENT (Teacher to choose the most applicable environment)  
Natural environment: school surrounding, play-ground… 
Artificial environment: classroom, pictures, school hall… (classroom is applicable for this 
lesson) 
Technological environment: e-learning websites… (radio, projector, computers, etc are 
applicable for this lesson) 
TEACHING AND LEARNING MATERIALS/RESOURCES: radio, computer, projector, 
recorded poems, flip charts and markers. 
 
EXPECTED STANDARD:   Understanding of poetry demonstrated adequately. 
 
 
 
LESSON PROGRESSION: 
STAGES 
TEACHER’S ROLE 
 
LEARNERS’ ROLE 
 
PERFORMANCE 
INDICATOR

25 
 
INTRODUCTION 
 
   (5 minutes) 
 Introduce the lesson 
by asking learners 
what 
they 
know 
about poetry.  
 Give responses about 
what poetry is. 
 
 
 
Learners say what 
they 
know 
about 
poetry. 
 
DEVELOPMENT 
 
Step 1:  
Group work 
 
(15 Minutes) 
 Divide learners in 
groups and share 
excerpts of a poem 
written on A4 papers. 
For example, ‘Zambia 
is Getting Warmer’ and 
let them read it. 
 Take-turns to read the 
poem in groups. 
Learners take-turns 
reading the poem. 
 
Step 2: 
Class discussion 
  
 (15 Minutes) 
 Lead a class discussion 
on what a poem is and 
its elements (form, line 
and stanza) 
 Actively take part in the 
discussion of what a 
poem is and its elements 
(form, line and stanza)  
 Learners take note 
of the elements of 
poetry, which are 
form, 
line 
and 
stanza.  
 
 
Step 3:  
Class discussion 
 
 (10 Minutes) 
 
 Consolidate the lesson 
by highlighting key 
aspects of poetry and 
its elements as 
explained by the 
learners. 
 
 Explain poetry and its 
elements (form, line and 
stanza): 
 Poetry- is a form of 
literature that uses 
rhythmic and figurative 
language to express 
emotions and ideas. 
 A poem is a literary 
work that expresses 
emotions and ideas.  
 Poems are usually 
Didactic (teach) or 
Aesthetic (beauty). 
 Form- the structure of a 
poem and collection of 
lines with a metrical 
rhythm in a poem.  
 Stanza: a division of a 
poem consisting of two 
or more lines arranged 
together as a unit. 
 Line: a unit of writing 
into which a poem or 
play is divided. 
 
Learners 
explain 
poetry 
and 
its 
elements which are 
form, 
line 
and 
stanza.

26 
 
 
Step 4:  
Assessment 
Activity 
 
(30 Minutes) 
  
Home work 
 Provide a poem and ask 
learners to identify the 
elements of poetry in 
that poem: form, line 
and stanza. 
 
 
 Assign learners to go 
and research on the 
other elements of 
poetry: verse, rhyme 
and rhythm. 
 Individually write down 
the elements of poetry in 
the given poem: form, 
line and stanza. 
 
 
 Attend to the assigned 
homework on the other 
elements of poetry: 
verse, rhyme and 
rhythm. 
 
Learners give 
correct responses to 
the given exercise. 
 
 
 
 
Submission of 
homework in the 
next lesson. 
 
Conclusion  
(5 minutes) 
 Summarize the day’s 
lesson through question 
and answer such as: 
 What do you 
understand by the 
following elements of 
poetry: form, line and 
stanza? 
 
 Give answers to the 
posed questions 
highlighting the 
elements of poetry 
which are form, line and 
stanza. 
 Learners 
highlight 
what poetry is and 
its elements: form, 
line and stanza.
